title: Never Use [ ] as a Default Argument in Python...
summary: A video explaining why mutable objects like lists should not be used as default arguments in Python functions. This is a common gotcha where default mutable arguments are evaluated once at function definition time, leading to unexpected shared state across function calls.
writeup: A focused walkthrough of one of Python's most common silent bugs: mutable objects (lists, dicts, sets, and custom class instances) used as default function arguments are initialized once at function definition time, not on each call, so all callers share the same underlying object. The video demonstrates the problem with a game-inventory example, then shows two robust fixes. The core lesson is that default argument initialization is a property of the function object itself, not of any individual call.

key_points: When a mutable object such as an empty list is written directly as a default argument, Python creates that object once when the function is defined; every subsequent call that omits the argument receives the same object, causing state to leak across calls.
The bug appears identically for lists, dicts, sets, and user-defined class instances used as defaults, because all of these are mutable and initialized at definition time.
The standard fix is to default the parameter to None and initialize the mutable object inside the function body, so each call that omits the argument gets a fresh object rather than a shared one.

When a pre-built mutable object must be passed as a default, calling inventory.copy() (or the equivalent for other types) inside the function ensures each caller gets an independent copy rather than a reference to the shared original.
The REPL provides a quick way to observe the bug: define a function with an empty list default, call .append inside it, and call it repeatedly to watch the list grow across calls.

chapters.title: Same Problem With Dicts and Sets
